ac:rich-text-body.p: The SKA offers multiple proposal types to accommodate different science needs and timescales. This page describes each type, eligibility criteria, review processes, and typical time allocations.

p: The SKA recognizes three main proposal types, each designed for different observing scenarios and science goals:

ul.li: PI Projects: Standard, competitively reviewed observing programmes (available Cycle 0 onwards)
Director's Discretionary Time (DDT): Rapid-response observations for time-critical targets (available Cycle 0 onwards)
Key Science Projects (KSP): Large, long-duration programmes spanning multiple cycles (planned for Cycle 3 onwards)

p: PI Projects are the standard avenue for SKA observations. They are competitively peer-reviewed and awarded observing time based on scientific merit.

ul.li: Open to all researchers: Scientists from any institution worldwide can serve as PI
Institutional affiliation: PI must have a formal affiliation with a research or educational institution
No nationality restrictions: Eligibility is based on institutional affiliation, not citizenship
Early-career researchers: Special consideration or reserved time available; check Cycle Announcement for details
Typical length: 5–10 pages (including science case, technical justification, figures)
Structured format: Title, abstract, science category, team, observing requirements, technical specifications, data management
Single-cycle scope: One observing cycle only (typically 12 months)
Peer review: Proposals are evaluated by independent experts in relevant science areas
Science committee: SKA Science Collaboration committees review proposals and make time-allocation recommendations
Time Allocation Committee (TAC): Final decisions on observing time are made by the TAC, which balances scientific merit against available resources
Review timeline: Typically 2–3 months from proposal deadline to notification of results
Typical awards: 5–1000 hours (depending on science case and cycle constraints)
Average award: ~50–200 hours per proposal (highly variable)
Total per cycle: ~60–70% of available observing time allocated to PI projects; remainder reserved for DDT, calibration, commissioning
Proprietary period: Typically 12 months from observation date; PI has exclusive access during this period
Release to archive: After proprietary period, data enters SKA data archive available to all users
Extended proprietary period: May be requested for special cases (e.g., sensitive targets); must be justified in proposal
Publication rights: PI team has right of first publication during proprietary period
SKA publication policy: All data users (including archive users post-proprietary period) must acknowledge the SKA in publications
Collaboration statement: PIs should acknowledge co-investigators and collaborators

p: DDT is allocated by the Director of the SKA for rapid-response observations of unexpected or time-critical targets. Observations typically occur within days to weeks of approval.

ul.li: Science-driven urgency: Target must be scientifically time-critical (e.g., transient, ToO, coordinated multi-wavelength campaign)
Discovery context: Often triggered by discoveries from other telescopes (GRBs, transient alerts, gravitational-wave events)
Any researcher: Similar to PI projects; no career-stage restrictions
Brief proposal: 1–2 pages, with emphasis on urgency and time-critical nature
Rapid turnaround: Can be submitted anytime (no fixed deadline)
Fast review: Typically reviewed within 24–48 hours
Expedited evaluation: DDT proposals are evaluated on scientific merit and feasibility, with emphasis on rapid decision-making
Director's decision: Final approval rests with the SKA Director or delegated authority
Scheduling priority: Approved DDT observations may displace non-critical programmes in the schedule
Per-proposal awards: Typically 1–100 hours
Annual DDT pool: ~10–15% of observing time reserved for DDT across all cycles
Competition: Fewer proposals compete for DDT time, but only high-priority targets are approved
Proprietary period: Same as PI projects (typically 12 months)
Publication policy: Same as PI projects
Rapid publication encouraged: Given the time-critical nature, PIs are encouraged to publish results quickly
GRB follow-up: Rapid imaging/spectroscopy of gamma-ray burst afterglows
Gravitational-wave event localization: Counterpart identification following LIGO/Virgo detections
Pulsar magnetar outbursts: Observations of sudden emission changes or glitches
Nearby supernova discovery: Rapid characterization of newly discovered SNe
Solar flare monitoring: Real-time observation of significant solar radio events

p: Key Science Projects (KSPs) are large, coordinated observing programmes spanning multiple observing cycles (typically 2–5 years). KSPs are designed to tackle major science questions and require substantial array time.

ul.li: Major science drivers: Address key science goals outlined in the SKA Science Book (e.g., "Detecting the Epoch of Reionization," "A Census of Pulsars in the Galactic Plane")
Large data volumes: Require multi-year surveys or deep, targeted observations
Team effort: Coordinated science teams with expertise spanning multiple areas
Strategic importance: Align with SKA's mission to pursue transformative astronomy
Large consortia: Must involve a collaboration of >~10 researchers from multiple institutions
Formal structure: Governance model defining decision-making, publication rights, and data-sharing within the collaboration
Science leadership: Identified science lead and co-leads for different working groups
Comprehensive proposal: 20–40 pages (much longer than PI projects or DDT)
Sections include: Detailed science rationale, survey strategy, data products, team organization, management plan, budget (if applicable)
Multi-cycle specification: Year-by-year breakdown of observations and expected milestones
Science review: Detailed evaluation by science committee experts
Management review: Assessment of collaboration structure, team capability, and feasibility
Strategic alignment: Evaluation of how the KSP aligns with SKA's strategic science goals
Timeline: Typically 3–4 months for review and decision
Substantial allocations: Typically 1,000–10,000+ hours per year
Multi-year commitment: Time guaranteed across multiple cycles (subject to milestone review)
Annual reviews: KSPs are reviewed annually to assess progress and adjust future allocations if needed
Total annual pool: ~20–30% of available observing time reserved for KSPs (starting Cycle 3)
Proprietary period: Typically 18–24 months for KSP data (longer than PI projects to allow coordinated analysis)
Collaboration data policy: Each KSP defines its own publication policy and data-sharing rules among collaborators
Public release: After proprietary period, all KSP data enters SKA archive
Coordinated publication: KSPs coordinate major publications to avoid conflicting claims and maximize impact
HI Universe Survey (HUS): Deep HI survey for galaxy evolution and cosmology across multiple redshift bins
Galactic Plane Survey: Comprehensive continuum and spectral-line survey of the Milky Way
Pulsar Timing Array: Multi-year timing of hundreds of pulsars for gravitational-wave detection
AGN and Jet Population Study: Statistical survey and detailed follow-up of active galaxies and relativistic jets
Your science can be completed in a single observing cycle (12 months)
You have a well-defined science question with clear observing requirements
Your team is small to moderate in size (<~20 researchers)
Your target is time-critical (transient, GRB, gravitational-wave counterpart, pulsar outburst)
You need observing time within weeks (not during scheduled Cycle)
You can justify scientific urgency to the Director
Your science requires multi-year observations (e.g., survey, monitoring)
You have or can assemble a large, coordinated collaboration
Your science aligns with SKA's strategic science goals
You need substantial array time (1000+ hours/year)

p: Detailed submission instructions and deadlines are provided in the annual SKA Cycle Call for Proposals, published 6–9 months before observations begin. The Cycle Call includes:

ul.li: Proposal deadlines (PI and KSP)
Available observing time per cycle
Proposal template and submission system details
Special programmes or reserved time (e.g., for early-career researchers, commissioning science)
Contact information for User Support

ac:rich-text-body.p: The SKA User Support team is available to help with proposal preparation. Contact them early to discuss feasibility, technical requirements, or proposal strategy. Pre-submission consultations can improve proposal quality and success rates.

ul.li: Feedback available: All proposers receive reviewer comments (typically 1–2 months after decision)
Appeals process: If you believe a proposal was treated unfairly, an appeals process is available; details in Cycle Call
Resubmission: Proposals are often submitted multiple times; resubmit with attention to reviewer comments for improved chances

ac:rich-text-body.p: Contact the SKA User Support team at proposals@skao.int or visit the SKA website for the latest Cycle Call and proposal guidelines.
